Titanic Survival Analysis

xipecast

2026

Overview

Analysis of 891 Titanic passengers to determine survival factors.

  • Rows: 891 passengers
  • Columns: 12 variables
  • Target: Survival (0=Died, 1=Survived)

1. Overall Survival

titanic_clean %>%
  mutate(Survived = ifelse(Survived == 1, "Survived", "Died")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Survived, fill = Survived)) +
  geom_bar() +
  labs(title = "Titanic Survival Count", x = "Status", y = "Count")

2. Survival by Gender

titanic_clean %>%
  mutate(Survived = ifelse(Survived == 1, "Survived", "Died")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Sex, fill = Survived)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  labs(title = "Survival by Gender", x = "Gender", y = "Count")

Gender Survival Rates

titanic_clean %>%
  group_by(Sex) %>%
  summarise(
    Total = n(),
    Survived = sum(Survived),
    Rate = paste0(round(Survived/Total * 100, 1), "%")
  )
## # A tibble: 2 × 4
##   Sex    Total Survived Rate 
##   <chr>  <int>    <dbl> <chr>
## 1 female   314      233 74.2%
## 2 male     577      109 18.9%

3. Survival by Class

titanic_clean %>%
  mutate(Survived = ifelse(Survived == 1, "Survived", "Died")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(Pclass), fill = Survived)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  labs(title = "Survival by Class", x = "Class (1=First, 2=Second, 3=Third)", y = "Count")

Class Survival Rates

titanic_clean %>%
  group_by(Pclass) %>%
  summarise(
    Total = n(),
    Survived = sum(Survived),
    Rate = paste0(round(Survived/Total * 100, 1), "%")
  )
## # A tibble: 3 × 4
##   Pclass Total Survived Rate 
##    <dbl> <int>    <dbl> <chr>
## 1      1   216      136 63%  
## 2      2   184       87 47.3%
## 3      3   491      119 24.2%

4. Combined Gender + Class

titanic_clean %>%
  group_by(Sex, Pclass) %>%
  summarise(
    Total = n(),
    Survived = sum(Survived),
    Rate = paste0(round(Survived/Total * 100, 1), "%")
  )
## `summarise()` has regrouped the output.
## ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by Sex and Pclass.
## ℹ Output is grouped by Sex.
## ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
## ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(Sex, Pclass))` for per-operation grouping
##   (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.
## # A tibble: 6 × 5
## # Groups:   Sex [2]
##   Sex    Pclass Total Survived Rate 
##   <chr>   <dbl> <int>    <dbl> <chr>
## 1 female      1    94       91 96.8%
## 2 female      2    76       70 92.1%
## 3 female      3   144       72 50%  
## 4 male        1   122       45 36.9%
## 5 male        2   108       17 15.7%
## 6 male        3   347       47 13.5%
titanic_clean %>%
  group_by(Sex, Pclass) %>%
  summarise(
    survival_rate = round(sum(Survived)/n() * 100, 1)
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(Pclass), y = survival_rate, fill = Sex)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(survival_rate, "%")),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.9),
            vjust = -0.5) +
  labs(title = "Survival Rate by Gender & Class",
       subtitle = "Gender mattered more than class",
       x = "Class", y = "Survival Rate (%)")
## `summarise()` has regrouped the output.
## ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by Sex and Pclass.
## ℹ Output is grouped by Sex.
## ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
## ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(Sex, Pclass))` for per-operation grouping
##   (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.

5. Survival by Age

titanic_clean %>%
  mutate(Survived = ifelse(Survived == 1, "Survived", "Died")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Age, fill = Survived)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, position = "dodge") +
  labs(title = "Survival by Age", x = "Age", y = "Count")

6. Fare vs Survival

titanic_clean %>%
  mutate(Survived = ifelse(Survived == 1, "Survived", "Died")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Survived, y = Fare, fill = Survived)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(title = "Fare vs Survival", x = "Status", y = "Fare Paid")

Key Findings

Factor Finding
Gender Women survived at 74.2% vs men at 18.9%
Class 1st class 63% vs 3rd class 24.2%
Combined Rich women = 96.8% survival, Poor men = 13.5%
Age Mean age deaths (30.6) vs survived (28.3)
Fare Higher fare = better survival odds

Conclusion

A passenger’s best chance of survival was being female and first class. Poor males in 3rd class had only a 13.5% chance of survival. Gender was a stronger predictor than class or age.
